_comment: DIALECTICA Theory Frameworks — 15 conflict resolution frameworks as TheoryFrameworkNode seeds

key_propositions: Conflicts escalate in predictable stages from mild tension to mutual destruction
Each stage requires qualitatively different intervention strategies
Stage determines whether parties can self-resolve or need third-party help
Escalation is reversible through targeted de-escalation interventions
Crossing into Lose-Lose zone requires external coercive intervention
escalation_indicators: Hardening of positions
Shift from debate to action
Coalition formation
Dehumanization of opponent
Threats and counter-threats
Physical violence or sabotage
de_escalation_levers: Facilitate dialogue at stages 1-3
Introduce mediator at stages 4-6
Apply arbitration or judicial intervention at stages 7-9
Rebuild communication channels
Separate parties physically if violence present
primary_questions: What stage is the conflict at on Glasl's 9-stage scale?
Is the conflict in Win-Win, Win-Lose, or Lose-Lose zone?
What type of third-party intervention is appropriate at this stage?
What events triggered escalation between stages?
Is de-escalation toward a lower stage possible?
citations: Glasl, F. (1999). Confronting Conflict. Hawthorn Press.
Glasl, F. (1980). Konfliktmanagement. Haupt Verlag.

key_propositions: Conflicts become ripe for resolution when parties perceive a Mutually Hurting Stalemate
MHS alone is insufficient — a perceived Way Out must also exist
Ripeness is a subjective perception, not an objective condition
The ripe moment is perishable: it must be seized when it appears
MEO can substitute for or reinforce MHS as a trigger for negotiation
escalation_indicators: Parties not yet perceiving mutual cost (conflict not ripe)
One party believes military solution is still possible
No mutually acceptable formula for resolution visible
Absence of credible mediator or facilitator
de_escalation_levers: Document and communicate MHS to both parties
Identify and amplify MEO (attractive peace dividend)
Provide a credible formula for settlement
Introduce trusted mediator to hold the ripe moment
Create hurting costs for spoilers blocking negotiation
primary_questions: Is there a Mutually Hurting Stalemate — are both parties paying unacceptable costs?
Do parties perceive a Mutually Enticing Opportunity for resolution?
Is there a visible Way Out — an acceptable negotiated formula?
Is the moment currently ripe, pre-ripe, or post-ripe?
Which party still believes a unilateral solution is possible?
citations: Zartman, I.W. (1989). Ripe for Resolution. Oxford University Press.
Zartman, I.W. (2000). Ripeness: The Hurting Stalemate and Beyond. In Stern & Druckman (Eds.), International Conflict Resolution After the Cold War.

core_concepts: BATNA (Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement)
key_propositions: Durable agreements address underlying interests, not stated positions
Knowing your BATNA determines your negotiating power
Agreements within the ZOPA are achievable; outside it are not
Relationship repair and substantive resolution should be pursued simultaneously
Agreements evaluated against objective criteria are more stable
escalation_indicators: Positional bargaining hardening
Parties conflating relationship issues with substantive issues
No shared understanding of underlying interests
Absence of objective criteria for evaluation
Parties unaware of their own or opponent's BATNA
de_escalation_levers: Interest mapping — ask 'why' behind stated positions
BATNA clarification for both parties
Brainstorming options before evaluating them
Introducing objective external criteria
Separating relationship repair from deal negotiation
primary_questions: What are each party's underlying interests beneath their stated positions?
What is each party's BATNA?
Is there a ZOPA — do the reservation values overlap?
What objective criteria could evaluate proposed solutions?
Are parties conflating personal attacks with substantive disagreement?
citations: Fisher, R. & Ury, W. (1981). Getting to Yes. Houghton Mifflin.
Ury, W. (1993). Getting Past No. Bantam Books.

key_propositions: Conflict has three dimensions: attitudes, behaviors, and underlying contradictions
Structural violence (built into systems) is as harmful as direct violence
Cultural violence legitimizes structural and direct violence
Negative peace (absence of war) is insufficient — positive peace requires justice
Sustainable resolution requires addressing all three corners of the ABC triangle
escalation_indicators: Rising hostility or dehumanization in attitudes
Increasingly aggressive behaviors (threats, violence)
Structural inequalities worsening
Cultural narratives legitimizing violence
No progress on underlying contradiction (C)
de_escalation_levers: Address structural inequalities directly
Counter cultural narratives legitimizing violence
Change hostile attitudes through dialogue
Stop violent behaviors through ceasefire or enforcement
Resolve the underlying contradiction (C)
primary_questions: What is the underlying contradiction (C) driving the conflict?
What attitudes (A) sustain the conflict on each side?
What behaviors (B) express the conflict?
Is structural violence present — inequality built into systems?
What would positive peace (not just absence of violence) look like?
citations: Galtung, J. (1969). Violence, Peace, and Peace Research. Journal of Peace Research, 6(3).
Galtung, J. (1990). Cultural Violence. Journal of Peace Research, 27(3).

key_propositions: Conflict transformation changes the relationships and structures underlying conflict, not just the immediate dispute
Moral imagination — the capacity to envision constructive responses — is the heart of peacebuilding
Sustainable peace requires engagement at top, middle, and grassroots leadership levels
Elicitive approaches draw on local knowledge rather than imposing external models
The web of relationships is the locus of both conflict and transformation
escalation_indicators: Focus on transaction rather than transformation
Exclusion of middle-range leaders from peace processes
Grassroots communities not engaged in peace
Local knowledge and resources ignored
Relationship repair neglected in favor of formal agreements
de_escalation_levers: Engage top, middle, and grassroots leadership simultaneously
Build on local cultural resources for peace
Focus on transforming relationships not just resolving disputes
Cultivate moral imagination through dialogue
Create platforms for sustained relationship-building
primary_questions: What structural and relational changes would constitute transformation?
Are all three levels of leadership (top, middle, grassroots) engaged?
What local cultural resources support peacebuilding?
Is the process addressing the web of relationships or just the presenting issue?
Is moral imagination visible — can parties envision a different future?
citations: Lederach, J.P. (1997). Building Peace: Sustainable Reconciliation in Divided Societies. USIP Press.
Lederach, J.P. (2005). The Moral Imagination: The Art and Soul of Building Peace. Oxford University Press.

key_propositions: Conflicts pass through identifiable phases: emergence, escalation, de-escalation, settlement, transformation
Constructive conflict can produce positive outcomes including social change
De-escalation requires changes in grievances, relationships, or context
Settlements are more durable when parties feel ownership over the process
Transformation involves fundamental change in conflict relationships beyond settlement
escalation_indicators: Rapid phase escalation without pause
Settlement attempts failing repeatedly
Parties locked in de-escalation phase without moving to settlement
Spoilers actively preventing transformation
External actors prolonging conflict through arms or support
de_escalation_levers: Identify and address grievances driving emergence
Introduce confidence-building measures
Facilitate transition from de-escalation to settlement
Create ownership through participatory process design
Address spoilers through inclusion or isolation
primary_questions: What phase is the conflict currently in (emergence, escalation, de-escalation, settlement, transformation)?
What triggered emergence of this conflict?
What is preventing transition to the next phase?
Are there spoilers blocking settlement or transformation?
What confidence-building measures could accelerate de-escalation?
citations: Kriesberg, L. (1998). Constructive Conflicts: From Escalation to Resolution. Rowman & Littlefield.
Kriesberg, L. & Dayton, B. (2012). Constructive Conflicts (4th ed.). Rowman & Littlefield.

key_propositions: Cooperation occurs when parties have promotively interdependent goals — one's gain helps the other
Competition occurs when parties have contriently interdependent goals — one's gain hurts the other
The Crude Law: cooperation begets cooperation; competition begets competition
Cooperative contexts produce better outcomes than competitive ones for complex problems
Third-party framing of the situation as cooperative or competitive shapes behavior
escalation_indicators: Parties framing situation as zero-sum
Competitive dynamics established early in interaction
Absence of shared superordinate goals
Negative attribution of opponent's cooperative moves
Resource scarcity framing entrenching competition
de_escalation_levers: Reframe situation as promotively interdependent
Identify superordinate goals shared by both parties
Small cooperative acts to shift relational dynamic
Positive attribution training for opponent's moves
Restructure incentives to reward cooperation
primary_questions: Are the parties' goals promotively or contriently interdependent?
How are parties framing the situation — cooperative or competitive?
Are there superordinate goals that could reframe the relationship?
Is the Crude Law operating — is competition self-reinforcing?
What would shift the relational dynamic from competitive to cooperative?
citations: Deutsch, M. (1973). The Resolution of Conflict. Yale University Press.
Deutsch, M. (1949). A theory of cooperation and competition. Human Relations, 2(2).

key_propositions: Conflict behavior varies on two dimensions: assertiveness and cooperativeness
Five distinct conflict modes emerge from combinations of these dimensions
No single mode is universally best — appropriateness depends on context
Collaborating is highest value for complex problems requiring creative solutions
Habitual reliance on one mode is a conflict management failure
escalation_indicators: Parties locked in Competing mode escalating to coercion
Avoiding mode allowing problems to fester
Accommodating mode creating resentment through asymmetric concession
Compromising mode failing to address root causes
Absence of Collaborating mode for complex issues
de_escalation_levers: Map each party's dominant conflict mode
Introduce Collaborating mode for complex substantive issues
Use Accommodating mode strategically for low-stakes issues
Use Competing mode only for emergency or core values
Coach parties to expand their conflict mode repertoire
primary_questions: What conflict mode is each party habitually using?
Is the mode appropriate to the stakes and relationship?
Is Collaborating being used where creative solutions are needed?
Is Avoiding allowing unresolved issues to accumulate?
What mode would best serve the relationship long-term?
citations: Thomas, K.W. & Kilmann, R.H. (1974). Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument. Xicom.
Thomas, K.W. (1992). Conflict and Negotiation Processes in Organizations. In Handbook of Industrial and Organizational Psychology.

key_propositions: Social power derives from six distinct bases with different durability and side effects
Coercive and reward power require surveillance; referent and expert power do not
Referent power is the most durable because it is internalized by the target
Power imbalances shape whether parties can negotiate on equal terms
Legitimate power loses effectiveness when the authority structure is challenged
escalation_indicators: Heavy reliance on coercive power escalating resistance
Reward power withdrawal triggering retaliation
Expert power monopoly creating resentment
Legitimate power being contested
Referent power eroding through scandal or failure
de_escalation_levers: Shift from coercive to legitimate or referent power
Power-balancing through coalition support for weaker party
Build expert power through demonstrated competence
Restore legitimate power through procedural fairness
Cultivate referent power through relationship investment
primary_questions: What power bases does each party hold?
Is there a significant power asymmetry between parties?
Which power bases are being exercised and which are latent?
Is coercive power triggering backlash or resistance?
How durable is each party's power base?
citations: French, J.R.P. & Raven, B. (1959). The Bases of Social Power. In D. Cartwright (Ed.), Studies in Social Power.
Raven, B.H. (1993). The Bases of Power. Journal of Social Issues, 49(4).

key_propositions: Trust is a function of three perceived factors: ability, benevolence, and integrity
Low trust on any single factor (especially integrity) undermines overall trust
Propensity to trust is a trait that varies by individual and moderates trust formation
Trust enables risk-taking in relationships; distrust triggers self-protective behaviors
Trust repair after violation requires explicit action on the violated dimension
escalation_indicators: Trust breakdown along ability dimension (perceived incompetence)
Trust breakdown along benevolence dimension (malicious intent perceived)
Trust breakdown along integrity dimension (dishonesty or broken promises)
Propensity to trust very low — generalized distrust
Absence of trust-building experiences or shared history
de_escalation_levers: Demonstrate competence to rebuild ability-based trust
Signal genuine concern for other party to rebuild benevolence trust
Fulfill commitments consistently to rebuild integrity trust
Create low-stakes opportunities for successful cooperation
Third-party vouching to bootstrap initial trust
primary_questions: What is the overall trust level between parties?
Which trust dimension (ability, benevolence, integrity) is most deficient?
Has trust been violated — and on which dimension?
What is each party's propensity to trust as a baseline?
What trust-building actions are feasible at this stage?
citations: Mayer, R.C., Davis, J.H. & Schoorman, F.D. (1995). An Integrative Model of Organizational Trust. Academy of Management Review, 20(3).
Schoorman, F.D., Mayer, R.C. & Davis, J.H. (2007). An Integrative Model of Organizational Trust: Past, Present, and Future.

key_propositions: Eight primary emotions (joy, trust, fear, surprise, sadness, disgust, anger, anticipation) underlie all emotional experience
Emotions exist on an intensity continuum from mild to extreme
Opposite emotions cannot be simultaneously experienced at high intensity
Conflict emotion profiles predict party behavior and receptivity to resolution
Emotion dyads (mixtures of primary emotions) create complex conflict-relevant states
escalation_indicators: High-intensity anger escalating to rage
Fear intensifying to terror — triggering flight or freeze
Disgust deepening to loathing — dehumanization risk
Sadness deepening to grief — withdrawal from engagement
Asymmetric emotion profiles between parties preventing empathy
de_escalation_levers: Emotion validation before substantive engagement
De-intensification from extreme to milder emotion variants
Shift from anger to sadness (more amenable to dialogue)
Introduce anticipation (hope) as counterweight to fear
Create shared emotional experiences to build trust
primary_questions: What primary emotions are active for each party?
What is the intensity level of each emotion?
Are emotions asymmetric between parties (preventing empathy)?
Are disgust or contempt present (highest barriers to dialogue)?
What emotional state would enable productive engagement?
citations: Plutchik, R. (1980). Emotion: A Psychoevolutionary Synthesis. Harper & Row.
Plutchik, R. (2001). The Nature of Emotions. American Scientist, 89(4).

key_propositions: Causal claims require more than correlation — they require an interventional or counterfactual model
The causal hierarchy has three levels: seeing (association), doing (intervention), imagining (counterfactual)
Directed Acyclic Graphs make causal assumptions explicit and testable
Confounders must be identified and controlled to establish causal claims in conflict analysis
Counterfactual reasoning (what would have happened if X had not occurred) is essential for conflict attribution
escalation_indicators: Parties arguing from correlation not causation
Confounders unidentified in causal narratives
Counterfactuals being used rhetorically not analytically
Causal attribution errors driving blame dynamics
Intervention effects not distinguished from selection effects
de_escalation_levers: Build shared causal model through collaborative DAG construction
Identify confounders both parties agree are operating
Use counterfactual analysis to test proposed interventions
Separate causal from correlational claims in negotiations
Jointly evaluate intervention effects with do-calculus thinking
primary_questions: What causal mechanism links the identified trigger to the conflict outcome?
Is the causal claim associational, interventional, or counterfactual?
What confounders might be driving observed correlations?
What would have happened counterfactually if the key event had not occurred?
Can we build a shared DAG that both parties accept?
citations: Pearl, J. (2000). Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference. Cambridge University Press.
Pearl, J. & Mackenzie, D. (2018). The Book of Why. Basic Books.

key_propositions: Conflicts are constructed and sustained through the stories parties tell about them
Dominant conflict narratives become self-fulfilling through repetition and selective attention
Externalizing the problem (separating person from problem) opens space for change
Unique outcomes — exceptions to the dominant story — are the seeds of alternative narratives
Reauthoring involves co-creating a preferred story that is thicker and more complex
escalation_indicators: Rigid dominant narrative with no acknowledged exceptions
Total identification of person with problem behavior
Absence of unique outcomes or alternative accounts
Both parties telling incompatible totalizing stories
Media or public narrative reinforcing dominant conflict story
de_escalation_levers: Externalize the problem — give it a name separate from persons
Search for unique outcomes that contradict the dominant narrative
Ask questions that thicken the alternative story
Invite witnesses to validate the preferred alternative story
Help parties become authors of a preferred narrative
primary_questions: What is the dominant narrative each party tells about the conflict?
Are there unique outcomes — exceptions to the dominant story?
How is the problem externalized (or not) from the persons involved?
What alternative story is trying to emerge?
What audience reinforces the dominant narrative?
citations: Winslade, J. & Monk, G. (2000). Narrative Mediation: A New Approach to Conflict Resolution. Jossey-Bass.
Monk, G. & Winslade, J. (2013). When Stories Clash: Addressing Conflict with Narrative Mediation. Taos Institute Publications.

key_propositions: Dispute systems should prioritize interest-based procedures before rights and power procedures
Rights and power procedures are more costly and should be loop-back options not first resort
Low-cost rights and power procedures should be available when interests procedures fail
Prevention systems reduce dispute rates more effectively than reactive systems
System design should create motivation for parties to use interests procedures
escalation_indicators: Parties jumping to litigation or force without attempting interest-based resolution
Absence of accessible interest-based processes
Rights procedures being used for every minor dispute
No loop-back provisions from power back to interests
Dispute system designed by one party without the other's input
de_escalation_levers: Build accessible low-cost interests procedures as first step
Create loop-back provisions from arbitration/litigation to mediation
Design prevention mechanisms addressing root causes
Train parties in interest-based communication
Make rights and power procedures available but as last resort
primary_questions: What dispute procedures are currently in place?
Are parties going directly to rights or power procedures without trying interests first?
Are there loop-back provisions from rights/power back to interests?
Is there a prevention system addressing root causes?
Who designed the current dispute system — was it participatory?
citations: Ury, W., Brett, J.M. & Goldberg, S.B. (1988). Getting Disputes Resolved. Jossey-Bass.
Costantino, C. & Merchant, C. (1996). Designing Conflict Management Systems. Jossey-Bass.

key_propositions: Deep-rooted conflicts arise from denial of basic human needs (identity, security, recognition) that cannot be traded away
Unlike interests, basic human needs are non-negotiable and cannot be compromised
Provention — proactively removing conditions that frustrate needs — is more effective than prevention
Problem-solving workshops create conditions for parties to jointly analyze needs frustration
Sustainable resolution must address needs, not just interests or rights
escalation_indicators: Identity or recognition needs being denied or threatened
Security needs unmet — existential threat perceived
Parties treating non-negotiable needs as bargaining chips
Structural conditions systematically frustrating needs
Recognition and belonging needs denied by dominant group
de_escalation_levers: Identify which basic human needs are being frustrated for each party
Create conditions that satisfy basic needs without zero-sum tradeoffs
Problem-solving workshops to jointly analyze needs frustration
Structural change to remove needs-denying conditions
Recognize and validate identity needs explicitly
primary_questions: Which basic human needs (identity, security, recognition) are being frustrated?
Are the needs being treated as negotiable when they are actually non-negotiable?
What structural conditions are frustrating basic needs?
Is provention possible — can root causes be addressed before conflict intensifies?
Would a problem-solving workshop enable joint needs analysis?
citations: Burton, J. (1990). Conflict: Resolution and Provention. St. Martin's Press.
Burton, J. (1979). Deviance, Terrorism and War. St. Martin's Press.
